---
name: value-case-grill-2026-07-20
description: "Grilling session on synorg docs/value-case.md — decisions + findings, in progress"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 71960c4e-1e8a-4ef6-a286-8e810f13fe6a
  modified: 2026-07-20T15:00:31.146Z
---

Grilling `docs/value-case.md` (the pilot business case for a Head of Platform),
started 2026-07-20. One-question-at-a-time down the decision tree. **In progress.**

**Q1 — who holds the yes / which gate — LOCKED: (b)-with-a-twist.** Head of
Platform decides the *pilot* (cheap, their call); the doc's real job is arming them
for the *production* book-shrink-or-keep decision that **Finance** owns. Consequence:
the doc must survive a finance skeptic, and its finance framing belongs — but it
never says it's aimed at that downstream room, so it reads aimed at the wrong reader.

**Q2 — LOCKED: (a).** Gate the pitch on *"you cannot shrink the insurance"*
(p5/H100 surge floor is a one-way door), demote measurement to table stakes, promote
**the lever (safe lending)** to the product; add a §7 "why not a dashboard" bullet.
(The dashboard-is-cheap counter loses because the action it triggers — shrink — is
irreversible; lend-and-keep is the safe lever.)

**Q3 — OPEN. "Why are R&D and prod on separate GPU clusters in the real world?"**
(user-raised — it's the FOUNDATION the doc skips: it asserts the double-pay but never
justifies the separation, which is the first skeptic question). My ranked answer,
strongest→weakest justification for synorg: (1) **trust/tenancy boundary** — R&D
arbitrary code vs prod customer-data-under-compliance; shared GPU = VRAM residue +
blast-radius + compliance breach; THIS is what scrub-on-return exists to bridge, and
synorg's own machinery is the proof the boundary is structural; (2) reliability
posture (stable-reserved inference vs preemptible training); (3) workload/hardware
shape (latency+few-GPU vs throughput+big-NVLink/EFA); (4) **org/budget silos — the
WEAK one**, the CFO's "just merge the budgets + one pool" attack. Doc must prove
separation is (1)+(2)+(3) structural, NOT (4) — else fix is "pool it" and synorg is
over-build/dead-on-arrival. My rec = **(a)**: add a §0 "Why the clusters are separate
& can't be merged", trust-boundary as hero. Awaiting user pick (a/b/c) + whether the
4-reason ranking is complete.

**AWS GPU-availability evidence (web, 2026)** — searched to ground the load-bearing
premise, and it HARDENS it: on-demand H100 effectively sold out; Capacity
Reservations / Capacity Blocks the only guaranteed path (buy ahead, sell out weeks
out, non-interruptible); `InsufficientInstanceCapacity` is the normal P5 on-demand
experience; AWS hiking Capacity Block prices. Sources: SemiAnalysis GPU-shortage,
AWS re:Post p5.4xlarge, RONIN capacity-planning, Network World price-hike.

**NEW doc finding (fix-list)**: §8 go/no-go offers "shrink the book instead… a win,
not a failure" as clean/costless — but the README says *"released capacity may not
come back"* and the evidence confirms shrinking held GPU is a **one-way door**.
Internal contradiction: shrink treated as reversible in §8, irreversible in README.
This also *strengthens* synorg vs the measure-then-shrink counter (the action
measurement would trigger is itself irreversible → lend-and-keep is the safe lever).

**Fix-list so far** (not yet applied — grill still surfacing): (1) name the
downstream Finance audience in the doc; (2) close the "why not a dashboard" flank /
gate on can't-shrink-insurance; (3) §8 add caveat that shrinking held GPU is
irreversible.

**Q3 deepened (user push): "solve the contradiction any way OTHER than separating?"**
Reframed synorg as a TRIZ physical-contradiction resolution. Contradiction: same GPU
must be dedicated-to-prod (SLA+trust) AND used-by-R&D (or double-pay). Resolution menu:
SPACE=separate clusters (status quo synorg attacks); TIME=prod-by-day/train-by-night
(synorg core); CONDITION=Kueue priority-preemption (the "why not just Kueue"); SYSTEM/
SILICON=**MIG** (partition one H100 into isolated slices, simultaneous); MARKET=neocloud/
broker pools across tenants.
**Key insight**: synorg = TIME-separation + WARM-FLOOR(space) **hybrid**. Why not pure
CONDITION-preemption (simpler)? Because eviction isn't instant across the trust boundary
— training must checkpoint+drain+**scrub VRAM** before inference reuses the GPU, so you
can't preempt reactively fast enough for a hard SLA → must reclaim AHEAD of predicted
demand (before ramp), warm floor covers unpredictable spikes. The warm floor is the
spatial half of a deliberate hybrid, not a bolt-on — doc never states this.
**Three preconditions synorg needs (honest boundary)**: (1) GPU scarce (can't buy
on-demand — 2026 confirmed); (2) trust+SLA boundary real (can't naive-pool); (3) scrub+
checkpoint latency nonzero (can't preempt reactively). Remove any one → simpler
resolution wins: abundant→buy per-use; soft-boundary→Kueue pool; instant-scrub/MIG→
simultaneous silicon partition, no lending machine. **MIG + neoclouds are synorg's REAL
competitors — not the pure-spot/buy-more/do-nothing strawmen in §7.**
**Q3 rec (upgraded)**: (a) §0 = "the contradiction & why time-separation resolves it" +
fold in 3 preconditions + replace §7 strawmen with MIG+neocloud. Awaiting user pick +
whether hybrid framing matches their mental model + whether precondition-3 (MIG maturity)
is already shaky.

Remaining tree branches (not yet reached): the $350k anchor framing; break-even
inequality (§5) honesty; go/no-go trigger correctness; illustrative-numbers disclaimer
strength-or-dodge; **NEW: is precondition-3 (MIG can't do this yet) actually true in
2026 — needs verification, it's load-bearing.**

content.text:
Sharp — you've hit the actual load-bearing logic. Yes: if we hold the HW 24/7, there IS daytime capacity sitting there. But **daytime idle and overnight idle are different kinds of idle**, and that difference is the whole design.

## Held capacity splits three ways, not two
At any moment the held fleet is one of:
1. **Actively serving inference** — during the day this is most of it (the fleet is *sized for daytime peak* — that's its job).
2. **Surge headroom you must keep** — the warm floor. Held idle *on purpose* as insurance, because a spike can't be met by buying on demand (2026 evidence). This idle is **not recoverable — it's the product.**
3. **Safely-lendable idle** — capacity that's idle *and* will stay idle long enough to reclaim + scrub ahead of demand.

Your question is really: *"isn't there bucket-3 during the day too?"*

## Why daytime idle mostly isn't bucket-3
Daytime idle is **spike-prone and short-runway.** Lend it and a business-hours inference spike forces a *reactive* reclaim against live demand — and scrub latency (you must discard the instance before inference reuses the GPU) means you **can't reclaim fast enough** to protect a hard latency SLA. So daytime idle is unsafe to lend. It's therefore either:
- **bucket-2 (insurance)** — headroom you're keeping for the spike, or
- **oversizing** — genuine excess → that's a **shrink signal**, not a lending opportunity.

**This is the key move:** daytime idle is a *shrink-or-insurance* question, not a *lending* question. Overnight idle is the *lending* target because it's predictable and long-runway — hours before the morning ramp, so you reclaim **ahead** of demand, not reactively.

## So your logic, completed
"We hold the HW, so R&D could run daytime" → R&D running daytime = contending with **live inference** on the same GPUs = the unsafe condition-preemption case (constant preemption + scrub-latency SLA risk). That's *exactly why* R&D holds separate daytime capacity, and why synorg lends only the **safe** (overnight) idle. Holding the HW doesn't make daytime capacity *free to lend* — during the day it's either working, insuring, or oversized.

And the punchline that ties back to the value case: **if the pilot's `utilization-of-held` shows big persistent daytime idle, that's not "lend more" — it's "the book is too big, shrink it."** The measurement is what tells insurance from oversizing. That's the de-risking value from Q2, made concrete.

## The honest exception (your instinct isn't wrong)
*Predictable* daytime troughs — a single-region service with a timezone gap, a nightly-batch customer, a lunch dip — **are** safely lendable (predictable = reclaimable ahead). Overnight-only leaves those unclaimed. So "overnight-only" is the biggest *safe* block, not the *only* one — the daytime-predictable-trough lending is a real roadmap increment, gated on the measurement proving the trough is predictable enough.

## Grill finding (fix-list)
The value case must state the **three-bucket split** explicitly: held capacity is serving / insurance / safely-lendable, and **daytime idle is a shrink-or-insurance signal, not a lending target** — because the smart reader (you, in three questions) immediately asks "why not use the daytime idle?" Answering it converts the biggest apparent hole into the clearest statement of what synorg is: *it lends only the idle it can safely reclaim ahead of demand, and measures the rest so you can shrink it.*

---

Does the three-bucket framing (serving / insurance / safely-lendable) land? And — is your fleet's daytime demand actually *peak-flat*, or does it have **predictable troughs** (timezone gaps, batch customers) big enough that daytime-trough lending should move from roadmap to core?

---
name: value-case-grill-2026-07-20
description: "Grilling session on synorg docs/value-case.md — decisions + findings, in progress"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 71960c4e-1e8a-4ef6-a286-8e810f13fe6a
  modified: 2026-07-20T15:03:16.615Z
---

Grilling `docs/value-case.md` (the pilot business case for a Head of Platform),
started 2026-07-20. One-question-at-a-time down the decision tree. **In progress.**

**Q1 — who holds the yes / which gate — LOCKED: (b)-with-a-twist.** Head of
Platform decides the *pilot* (cheap, their call); the doc's real job is arming them
for the *production* book-shrink-or-keep decision that **Finance** owns. Consequence:
the doc must survive a finance skeptic, and its finance framing belongs — but it
never says it's aimed at that downstream room, so it reads aimed at the wrong reader.

**Q2 — LOCKED: (a).** Gate the pitch on *"you cannot shrink the insurance"*
(p5/H100 surge floor is a one-way door), demote measurement to table stakes, promote
**the lever (safe lending)** to the product; add a §7 "why not a dashboard" bullet.
(The dashboard-is-cheap counter loses because the action it triggers — shrink — is
irreversible; lend-and-keep is the safe lever.)

**Q3 — OPEN. "Why are R&D and prod on separate GPU clusters in the real world?"**
(user-raised — it's the FOUNDATION the doc skips: it asserts the double-pay but never
justifies the separation, which is the first skeptic question). My ranked answer,
strongest→weakest justification for synorg: (1) **trust/tenancy boundary** — R&D
arbitrary code vs prod customer-data-under-compliance; shared GPU = VRAM residue +
blast-radius + compliance breach; THIS is what scrub-on-return exists to bridge, and
synorg's own machinery is the proof the boundary is structural; (2) reliability
posture (stable-reserved inference vs preemptible training); (3) workload/hardware
shape (latency+few-GPU vs throughput+big-NVLink/EFA); (4) **org/budget silos — the
WEAK one**, the CFO's "just merge the budgets + one pool" attack. Doc must prove
separation is (1)+(2)+(3) structural, NOT (4) — else fix is "pool it" and synorg is
over-build/dead-on-arrival. My rec = **(a)**: add a §0 "Why the clusters are separate
& can't be merged", trust-boundary as hero. Awaiting user pick (a/b/c) + whether the
4-reason ranking is complete.

**AWS GPU-availability evidence (web, 2026)** — searched to ground the load-bearing
premise, and it HARDENS it: on-demand H100 effectively sold out; Capacity
Reservations / Capacity Blocks the only guaranteed path (buy ahead, sell out weeks
out, non-interruptible); `InsufficientInstanceCapacity` is the normal P5 on-demand
experience; AWS hiking Capacity Block prices. Sources: SemiAnalysis GPU-shortage,
AWS re:Post p5.4xlarge, RONIN capacity-planning, Network World price-hike.

**NEW doc finding (fix-list)**: §8 go/no-go offers "shrink the book instead… a win,
not a failure" as clean/costless — but the README says *"released capacity may not
come back"* and the evidence confirms shrinking held GPU is a **one-way door**.
Internal contradiction: shrink treated as reversible in §8, irreversible in README.
This also *strengthens* synorg vs the measure-then-shrink counter (the action
measurement would trigger is itself irreversible → lend-and-keep is the safe lever).

**Fix-list so far** (not yet applied — grill still surfacing): (1) name the
downstream Finance audience in the doc; (2) close the "why not a dashboard" flank /
gate on can't-shrink-insurance; (3) §8 add caveat that shrinking held GPU is
irreversible.

**Q3 deepened (user push): "solve the contradiction any way OTHER than separating?"**
Reframed synorg as a TRIZ physical-contradiction resolution. Contradiction: same GPU
must be dedicated-to-prod (SLA+trust) AND used-by-R&D (or double-pay). Resolution menu:
SPACE=separate clusters (status quo synorg attacks); TIME=prod-by-day/train-by-night
(synorg core); CONDITION=Kueue priority-preemption (the "why not just Kueue"); SYSTEM/
SILICON=**MIG** (partition one H100 into isolated slices, simultaneous); MARKET=neocloud/
broker pools across tenants.
**Key insight**: synorg = TIME-separation + WARM-FLOOR(space) **hybrid**. Why not pure
CONDITION-preemption (simpler)? Because eviction isn't instant across the trust boundary
— training must checkpoint+drain+**scrub VRAM** before inference reuses the GPU, so you
can't preempt reactively fast enough for a hard SLA → must reclaim AHEAD of predicted
demand (before ramp), warm floor covers unpredictable spikes. The warm floor is the
spatial half of a deliberate hybrid, not a bolt-on — doc never states this.
**Three preconditions synorg needs (honest boundary)**: (1) GPU scarce (can't buy
on-demand — 2026 confirmed); (2) trust+SLA boundary real (can't naive-pool); (3) scrub+
checkpoint latency nonzero (can't preempt reactively). Remove any one → simpler
resolution wins: abundant→buy per-use; soft-boundary→Kueue pool; instant-scrub/MIG→
simultaneous silicon partition, no lending machine. **MIG + neoclouds are synorg's REAL
competitors — not the pure-spot/buy-more/do-nothing strawmen in §7.**
**Q3 rec (upgraded)**: (a) §0 = "the contradiction & why time-separation resolves it" +
fold in 3 preconditions + replace §7 strawmen with MIG+neocloud. Awaiting user pick +
whether hybrid framing matches their mental model + whether precondition-3 (MIG maturity)
is already shaky.

**Q4-ish (user: "what happens during DAY when both prod+R&D need HW?")** — grounded
in schedule.yaml: lend window is **22:00–06:30 ONLY**; daytime the lent taint is OFF
and borrowingLimitCurve=0% → training NOT admitted to lendable pool in daylight →
lendable is prod's all day, R&D gets ZERO from it. **No daytime contention by design —
synorg sidesteps it, doesn't solve it.** Consequence: synorg monetizes the OVERNIGHT
idle only; kills the overnight slice of double-pay, not all of it; R&D daytime demand
out of scope (runs on R&D's own residual capacity or queues for 22:00). Rests on
time-asymmetry: inference diurnal (idle night) AND training time-flexible (batch,
tolerates "runs tonight"). Recoverable value = **overlap(inference-idle-hours,
R&D-time-flexible-backlog)**. This is the mechanism-level sharpening of §2's buried
"R&D backlog alignment" caveat.
**Fix-list add**: value case needs an explicit "What synorg does NOT do — daytime R&D
demand" section (recovers overnight idle only; daytime simultaneous demand still
separately provisioned). Honest boundary, pre-empts the first-5-minutes question.
**Open design fork (put to user, awaiting answer)**: overnight-only lend (current) vs
dynamic lending on ANY measured inference trough incl. daytime dips (more recovery, but
more reclaim churn/scrub cycles + daytime reclaim races live peak = tighter SLA risk).

Remaining tree branches (not yet reached): the $350k anchor framing; break-even
inequality (§5) honesty; go/no-go trigger correctness; illustrative-numbers disclaimer
strength-or-dodge; **precondition-3 (MIG can't do this yet) — verify, load-bearing**;
daytime-trough design fork resolution.

content.text:
Wait — that's the real unlock, and it breaks the value case's binary model. You've got **three** workload classes, not two:

Let me ground whether the platform even models three tiers or only two:

input.command: sed -n '1,80p' clusters/pilot/kueue/priorityclasses.yaml

content.content:
# PriorityClasses — the emergency reclaim layer that sits UNDER Kueue (KTD6).
# Kueue's borrowingLimit curve is the planned reclaim; when inference demand
# outruns the curve, these kube-scheduler priorities let a pending inference pod
# preempt training node-level with zero Kueue involvement — the render path never
# queues. (warm-floor-balloon, value -10, lives in
# clusters/pilot/karpenter/warm-floor-balloon.yaml — not duplicated here.)
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: inference-critical
value: 1000000
# Default policy: a pending inference pod preempts lower-priority pods (training)
# to schedule. This is the emergency layer of the render path (R1).
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority
globalDefault: false
description: "Customer inference (render path). Preempts training node-level when demand outruns the Kueue borrowing curve (KTD6)."
---
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: training-preemptible
value: 1000
# Training never preempts anything — it only ever soaks borrowed headroom and
# yields it (via checkpoint, KTD12) when inference reclaims. Never-preempt makes
# that one-directional: training waits for room, it never takes it.
preemptionPolicy: Never
globalDefault: false
description: "R&D training jobs. Preemptible, checkpoint-tolerant; never preempts (KTD12)."

content.text:
Confirmed at `priorityclasses.yaml`: the platform models **exactly two** classes — `inference-critical` (1000000) and `training-preemptible` (1000). Nothing between. So both the value case *and* the platform assume a binary. You just named the thing they're missing, and it's the deepest finding of this whole grill.

## Three workloads, two axes that matter
| Workload | SLA | Trust domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Realtime inference** (interactive) | hard (ms), spiky, diurnal | customer data |
| **Batch inference** (text-to-video) | soft (deadline mins–hrs), revenue | **customer data — SAME as realtime** |
| **R&D training** | softest (best-effort, internal) | **different — needs scrub** |

The two axes — SLA hardness *and* trust domain — don't move together. That's the whole point.

## The consequence that breaks the binary model
**Batch inference is the same trust domain as realtime.** So handing a node from batch-inference back to realtime needs **no scrub** — just checkpoint + requeue, fast. Which means:

- **Daytime troughs → batch inference.** Same domain, no scrub, fast preempt → *safe to soak daytime idle.* No lending layer needed — this is plain Kueue/kube-scheduler priority. This is the daytime idle you've been circling for three turns: it's not lost, it's **batch-inference's to fill.**
- **Overnight idle → R&D training.** Cross-domain, scrub required, slow reclaim → only safe with long runway → synorg's lending machinery, overnight only.

Realtime sits on top of both and preempts either.

## What this does to the economics (the big one)
The value case prices recovered idle at the **training-substitute price ($1.5–2.5/GPU-hr)** — the *lowest-value* filler. But the idle fills as a **priority stack by value**:

1. Realtime peaks (top)
2. **Batch inference** — priced at *customer revenue* (or avoided batch-capacity spend), and it's **endogenous, certain demand** if you have a t2v product — not the speculative R&D backlog §2 flags as "the single largest swing factor."
3. R&D training — the residual, priced at training-substitute.

**So the current value case describes only step 3** — the smallest, hardest, most-speculative slice — and omits steps 1–2 entirely. If batch inference demand is real, synorg's recoverable value is *materially larger and far more certain* than the 2–6% the doc computes, because you'd price the idle at batch-inference revenue, not training substitution.

## The product reframe
synorg isn't "lend inference GPUs to R&D overnight." It's a **SLA-tiered, trust-aware capacity fabric:**
1. **Soak idle with your own batch inference first** — same domain, just priorities, no scrub, daytime *and* overnight, revenue-priced. Cheapest and biggest.
2. **Lend the scrub-bounded residual to R&D overnight** — the cross-domain part that actually needs synorg's lending controller, ledger, and scrub.

**Lending is the last mile, not the whole story.** The novel, expensive machinery (scrub, staged reclaim, zero-net-release) is *only* required for the R&D cross-domain slice — the README currently over-applies "scrub every reclaim" to a batch-inference case that never needs it.

## Grill finding (the biggest fix)
- **Value case**: model three workloads; price recovered idle at the priority-stack (batch-inference revenue on top); lead with batch inference as the certain filler, R&D training as the residual.
- **Platform**: needs a **third priority class** — batch-inference, value between 1000 and 1000000, preemptible, same-trust-domain (no scrub). Today's two-tier stack can't express it. Real design increment, not a doc edit.

---

**The gating question — is batch inference actually a workload for the target?** You gave t2v as "say," so:

- **(a)** Yes, real and significant (t2v / async generation is a core product) → this is the **headline reframe**: batch inference is the primary idle-filler, R&D lending is the residual, and both value case *and* platform need the third tier. *(If true, this is bigger than everything else in the grill.)*
- **(b)** Maybe / small → footnote it: note the three-class model and that batch inference could soak daytime idle, but keep R&D-overnight as the pilot's headline.
- **(c)** No — the target is realtime-only inference + R&D → the binary stands, and this whole branch is out of scope.

Which is it — and if (a), does synorg's own product actually have batch inference today, or is this the target *customer's* workload mix?

interactive, R&D training") — BIGGEST FINDING OF THE GRILL.** Platform models only TWO
priority classes today (grounded: priorityclasses.yaml — inference-critical=1000000,
training-preemptible=1000, nothing between). Value case + platform both assume a binary;
reality is three. **Two axes that DON'T move together: SLA hardness AND trust domain.**
- Realtime inference: hard SLA (ms), spiky, diurnal, customer-data.
- Batch inference (t2v): soft SLA (deadline), revenue, **SAME trust domain as realtime**.
- R&D training: softest SLA, internal, **different domain → needs scrub**.
**Key consequence**: batch inference same-domain as realtime → NO scrub to hand a node
back → fast checkpoint+requeue preempt → batch inference SAFELY soaks DAYTIME troughs
with just priorities (no lending layer). R&D cross-domain → scrub → overnight lending
only. So the daytime-idle the user circled = batch-inference's to fill.
**Economic reframe (the big one)**: value case prices recovered idle at training-
substitute ($1.5-2.5/GPU-hr) = the LOWEST-value filler. Idle actually fills as a
priority STACK by value: realtime(top) → batch-inference(revenue-priced, ENDOGENOUS/
certain if t2v product exists — not the speculative R&D backlog §2 fears) → R&D
training(residual). **Current value case describes only the smallest/hardest/most-
speculative slice (step 3) and omits batch inference (steps 1-2).** If batch demand
real → recoverable value materially larger + far more certain.
**Product reframe**: synorg = SLA-tiered trust-aware capacity fabric: (1) soak idle with
own batch inference first — same domain, priorities only, no scrub, day+night, revenue-
priced (biggest+cheapest); (2) lend scrub-bounded residual to R&D overnight (the part
that actually needs synorg's controller/ledger/scrub). **Lending is the LAST MILE, not
the whole story.** README over-applies "scrub every reclaim" to batch-inference case
that never needs it.
**Fix-list**: value case model 3 workloads + price at priority-stack + lead with batch
inference as certain filler; PLATFORM needs a 3rd priority class (batch-inference, value
between 1000 and 1000000, preemptible, same-domain/no-scrub) — real design increment.
**GATING QUESTION awaiting user**: is batch inference real+significant for the target?
(a) yes → headline reframe, bigger than everything else in grill; (b) maybe/small →

---
name: value-case-grill-2026-07-20
description: "Grilling session on synorg docs/value-case.md — decisions + findings, in progress"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 71960c4e-1e8a-4ef6-a286-8e810f13fe6a
  modified: 2026-07-20T15:05:13.290Z
---

Grilling `docs/value-case.md` (the pilot business case for a Head of Platform),
started 2026-07-20. One-question-at-a-time down the decision tree. **In progress.**

**Q1 — who holds the yes / which gate — LOCKED: (b)-with-a-twist.** Head of
Platform decides the *pilot* (cheap, their call); the doc's real job is arming them
for the *production* book-shrink-or-keep decision that **Finance** owns. Consequence:
the doc must survive a finance skeptic, and its finance framing belongs — but it
never says it's aimed at that downstream room, so it reads aimed at the wrong reader.

**Q2 — LOCKED: (a).** Gate the pitch on *"you cannot shrink the insurance"*
(p5/H100 surge floor is a one-way door), demote measurement to table stakes, promote
**the lever (safe lending)** to the product; add a §7 "why not a dashboard" bullet.
(The dashboard-is-cheap counter loses because the action it triggers — shrink — is
irreversible; lend-and-keep is the safe lever.)

**Q3 — OPEN. "Why are R&D and prod on separate GPU clusters in the real world?"**
(user-raised — it's the FOUNDATION the doc skips: it asserts the double-pay but never
justifies the separation, which is the first skeptic question). My ranked answer,
strongest→weakest justification for synorg: (1) **trust/tenancy boundary** — R&D
arbitrary code vs prod customer-data-under-compliance; shared GPU = VRAM residue +
blast-radius + compliance breach; THIS is what scrub-on-return exists to bridge, and
synorg's own machinery is the proof the boundary is structural; (2) reliability
posture (stable-reserved inference vs preemptible training); (3) workload/hardware
shape (latency+few-GPU vs throughput+big-NVLink/EFA); (4) **org/budget silos — the
WEAK one**, the CFO's "just merge the budgets + one pool" attack. Doc must prove
separation is (1)+(2)+(3) structural, NOT (4) — else fix is "pool it" and synorg is
over-build/dead-on-arrival. My rec = **(a)**: add a §0 "Why the clusters are separate
& can't be merged", trust-boundary as hero. Awaiting user pick (a/b/c) + whether the
4-reason ranking is complete.

**AWS GPU-availability evidence (web, 2026)** — searched to ground the load-bearing
premise, and it HARDENS it: on-demand H100 effectively sold out; Capacity
Reservations / Capacity Blocks the only guaranteed path (buy ahead, sell out weeks
out, non-interruptible); `InsufficientInstanceCapacity` is the normal P5 on-demand
experience; AWS hiking Capacity Block prices. Sources: SemiAnalysis GPU-shortage,
AWS re:Post p5.4xlarge, RONIN capacity-planning, Network World price-hike.

**NEW doc finding (fix-list)**: §8 go/no-go offers "shrink the book instead… a win,
not a failure" as clean/costless — but the README says *"released capacity may not
come back"* and the evidence confirms shrinking held GPU is a **one-way door**.
Internal contradiction: shrink treated as reversible in §8, irreversible in README.
This also *strengthens* synorg vs the measure-then-shrink counter (the action
measurement would trigger is itself irreversible → lend-and-keep is the safe lever).

**Fix-list so far** (not yet applied — grill still surfacing): (1) name the
downstream Finance audience in the doc; (2) close the "why not a dashboard" flank /
gate on can't-shrink-insurance; (3) §8 add caveat that shrinking held GPU is
irreversible.

**Q3 deepened (user push): "solve the contradiction any way OTHER than separating?"**
Reframed synorg as a TRIZ physical-contradiction resolution. Contradiction: same GPU
must be dedicated-to-prod (SLA+trust) AND used-by-R&D (or double-pay). Resolution menu:
SPACE=separate clusters (status quo synorg attacks); TIME=prod-by-day/train-by-night
(synorg core); CONDITION=Kueue priority-preemption (the "why not just Kueue"); SYSTEM/
SILICON=**MIG** (partition one H100 into isolated slices, simultaneous); MARKET=neocloud/
broker pools across tenants.
**Key insight**: synorg = TIME-separation + WARM-FLOOR(space) **hybrid**. Why not pure
CONDITION-preemption (simpler)? Because eviction isn't instant across the trust boundary
— training must checkpoint+drain+**scrub VRAM** before inference reuses the GPU, so you
can't preempt reactively fast enough for a hard SLA → must reclaim AHEAD of predicted
demand (before ramp), warm floor covers unpredictable spikes. The warm floor is the
spatial half of a deliberate hybrid, not a bolt-on — doc never states this.
**Three preconditions synorg needs (honest boundary)**: (1) GPU scarce (can't buy
on-demand — 2026 confirmed); (2) trust+SLA boundary real (can't naive-pool); (3) scrub+
checkpoint latency nonzero (can't preempt reactively). Remove any one → simpler
resolution wins: abundant→buy per-use; soft-boundary→Kueue pool; instant-scrub/MIG→
simultaneous silicon partition, no lending machine. **MIG + neoclouds are synorg's REAL
competitors — not the pure-spot/buy-more/do-nothing strawmen in §7.**
**Q3 rec (upgraded)**: (a) §0 = "the contradiction & why time-separation resolves it" +
fold in 3 preconditions + replace §7 strawmen with MIG+neocloud. Awaiting user pick +
whether hybrid framing matches their mental model + whether precondition-3 (MIG maturity)
is already shaky.

**Q4-ish (user: "what happens during DAY when both prod+R&D need HW?")** — grounded
in schedule.yaml: lend window is **22:00–06:30 ONLY**; daytime the lent taint is OFF
and borrowingLimitCurve=0% → training NOT admitted to lendable pool in daylight →
lendable is prod's all day, R&D gets ZERO from it. **No daytime contention by design —
synorg sidesteps it, doesn't solve it.** Consequence: synorg monetizes the OVERNIGHT
idle only; kills the overnight slice of double-pay, not all of it; R&D daytime demand
out of scope (runs on R&D's own residual capacity or queues for 22:00). Rests on
time-asymmetry: inference diurnal (idle night) AND training time-flexible (batch,
tolerates "runs tonight"). Recoverable value = **overlap(inference-idle-hours,
R&D-time-flexible-backlog)**. This is the mechanism-level sharpening of §2's buried
"R&D backlog alignment" caveat.
**Fix-list add**: value case needs an explicit "What synorg does NOT do — daytime R&D
demand" section (recovers overnight idle only; daytime simultaneous demand still
separately provisioned). Honest boundary, pre-empts the first-5-minutes question.
**Open design fork (put to user, awaiting answer)**: overnight-only lend (current) vs
dynamic lending on ANY measured inference trough incl. daytime dips (more recovery, but
more reclaim churn/scrub cycles + daytime reclaim races live peak = tighter SLA risk).

**Q5 (user: "if we DON'T release HW, isn't it enough to run R&D daytime anyway?") —
THE CRUX. Answer = THREE-BUCKET split of held capacity:** (1) actively serving inference
(daytime = most of it, fleet sized for peak); (2) surge headroom you MUST keep = warm
floor = insurance, NOT recoverable, it's the product; (3) safely-lendable idle = idle
that stays idle long enough to reclaim+scrub AHEAD of demand. Daytime idle mostly is NOT
bucket-3: spike-prone + short-runway + scrub-latency → reactive reclaim can't protect a
hard SLA → daytime idle is either bucket-2 (insurance) OR oversizing=**SHRINK signal**,
never a lending target. Overnight idle IS bucket-3 (predictable, hours of runway to ramp
→ reclaim ahead). **Punchline tying to Q2 de-risking**: persistent daytime idle in
`utilization-of-held` = "shrink the book", not "lend more" — the measurement is what
tells insurance from oversizing. R&D running held-fleet daytime = contending with LIVE
inference = the unsafe condition-preemption case → that's WHY R&D holds separate daytime
capacity, WHY synorg lends only overnight. Honest exception: PREDICTABLE daytime troughs
(timezone gaps, batch customers) ARE safely lendable → daytime-trough roadmap increment,
gated on measurement proving predictability. **Fix-list add**: value case must state the
three-bucket split explicitly + "daytime idle = shrink-or-insurance signal, not a lending
target" — converts the biggest apparent hole into the clearest statement of what synorg
is. Awaiting user: does 3-bucket land + is their fleet peak-flat or has predictable
daytime troughs (decides if daytime-trough lending is roadmap vs core).

Remaining tree branches (not yet reached): the $350k anchor framing; break-even
inequality (§5) honesty; go/no-go trigger correctness; illustrative-numbers disclaimer
strength-or-dodge; **precondition-3 (MIG can't do this yet) — verify, load-bearing**;
daytime-trough design fork resolution.

message.content: consider that synorg services internal customer that provides services b2b

content: CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE (full). Drop articles/filler/pleasantries/hedging. Fragments OK. Code/commits/security: write normal.

content.text:
That reframes the stakeholder chain and, crucially, it *resolves* the gating question and adds value axes the doc completely misses.

Chain: **synorg (platform team, Head of Platform = buyer) → serves → internal BU → serves → external B2B customers.**

## What "internal customer, B2B" changes

**1. Batch inference is confirmed real → gating question = (a).** A B2B service almost always has both surfaces: realtime (interactive API to client businesses) *and* batch (async jobs clients submit — t2v, bulk generation). So the three-workload model isn't hypothetical — it's the internal customer's actual product shape. Batch inference is the **customer's** workload, and it's real. The headline reframe is live.

**2. The org-silo separation reason collapses — which is *good* for synorg.** One internal customer, likely one budget → you *can't* justify separate inference/training clusters by "different cost centers" (Q3's weak reason #4). So the separation **must** be structural (SLA + compliance + workload shape). That's exactly the argument you want: it forces the honest, defensible justification and kills the "just merge the budgets" CFO attack before it starts.

**3. The trust boundary gets STRONGER, but re-justified — compliance, not adversarial.** I had it slightly wrong earlier. Internal R&D isn't a *malicious* tenant, so the "untrusted code" framing weakens. But the B2B service serves **external customers' data under contract** (DPA/GDPR/SOC2). Training that could read residue of that customer-inference VRAM is a **contractual data-isolation breach** — even from your own internal R&D. So the scrub boundary is *very* real; its justification shifts from "defend against attacker" to **"provable contractual isolation."** Precondition #2 holds firmly.

## Two value axes the doc entirely misses (both specific to internal-B2B)

**A. The scrub + zero-net-release ledger is a COMPLIANCE ARTIFACT, not just a reliability mechanism.** For a B2B customer, "training provably never saw your inference data — here's the per-cycle ledger and the scrub proof" is something the BU can show *its* auditors and *its* clients. synorg's core mechanism doubles as an **auditable data-isolation control** for the B2B service's compliance posture. That's concrete B2B value the value case never claims.

**B. Utilization-of-held becomes internal chargeback/showback.** With an internal customer, the Q2 de-risking metric isn't just "measure to de-risk" — it's a **cost-allocation tool**. The platform team shows the BU exactly what its held fleet earns, allocates the $350k accordingly, and holds the BU accountable for its own sizing. De-risking → FinOps governance.

## Net reframe
The "double-pay" is now **entirely internal** (one customer holds an inference fleet *and* separately provisions training). synorg's real pitch: **collapse one internal B2B customer's three-SLA workload onto one held fleet** — batch inference soaks idle at revenue-adjacent value (no scrub, same domain), R&D training takes the scrub-bounded overnight residual, the scrub/ledger is the customer's compliance proof, and utilization-of-held is the chargeback lever. That's a materially bigger and more defensible story than "lend idle GPUs to R&D."

---

We've now surfaced a large, coherent fix-list across ~6 branches. Decision fork:

- **(a)** **Consolidate now** — the findings cohere into a value-case rewrite (three-workload model, three-bucket capacity, compliance + chargeback axes, MIG/neocloud alternatives, honest boundaries). I draft the restructured `value-case.md`; per your Opus-plan/Codex-execute rule I'd plan it first. *(My rec — the grill has hit diminishing returns; the insights want to land in the doc.)*
- **(b)** **Keep grilling** the remaining branches first — $350k anchor framing, the §5 break-even inequality, go/no-go triggers, illustrative-numbers-as-strength-or-dodge — then consolidate.
- **(c)** Something else — e.g., grill the *platform* gap (the missing third priority class) rather than the doc.

Which way?

content.text: Chose (a). Planning the rewrite first (per your Opus-plan / execute rule). Loading the approval gate.

ExitPlanMode is for code-implementation plans read from a plan file — this is a prose rewrite, so I'll present the plan inline and get your go directly. Cleaner, honors the tool's contract.

## Plan — rewrite `docs/value-case.md`

**Execution note first:** I recommend **I write this myself, not dispatch to Codex.** Your Opus-plan/Codex-execute rule targets *coding*; this is nuanced business-case prose that needs the full grill context (three-workload model, compliance/chargeback axes) and must dodge AI-writing-slop. Codex lacks the context and would flatten it. Plan stays with me, execution too. Flag if you disagree.

### New structure (current 8 §§ → 11 §§, ~2× length, restructured)

| § | Section | Source | Key change |
|---|---|---|---|
| intro | who + honest framing | keep | **name the downstream Finance audience** (fix 1) |
| 1 | **Workload reality: three classes, not two** | NEW | realtime / batch-inf / R&D on two axes (SLA × trust domain) (fix 7,8) |
| 2 | **The contradiction & how synorg resolves it** | NEW | dedicate-vs-utilize; resolution menu; time+warm-floor hybrid; why-not-preemption (scrub latency); 3 preconditions (fix 4) |
| 3 | The cost being attacked | §1 | reframe as *internal* double-pay; keep $350k anchor |
| 4 | **What synorg recovers: the priority stack** | §2 | three-bucket capacity (serving/insurance/lendable); idle fills by value realtime→batch-inf(revenue-priced)→R&D(residual); old table = the R&D residual slice, labeled as such (fix 6,7) |
| 5 | **What synorg does NOT do** | NEW | daytime R&D out of scope; daytime idle = shrink-or-insurance signal (fix 5) |
| 6 | What justifies the held book | §3 | **add compliance-isolation** (scrub+ledger as auditable control) (fix 8A) |
| 7 | Pilot's real value: de-risk + govern | §4 | gate on can't-shrink-insurance; measurement=table-stakes, lever=product; **add chargeback/showback** (fix 2,8B) |
| 8 | Break-even | §5 | batch-inf revenue on the left side; **shrink-is-irreversible caveat** (fix 3) |
| 9 | Levers | §6 | add batch-inf fraction + tier boundaries |
| 10 | Why not just… | §7 | **replace strawmen** (spot/buy-more) with real alternatives: MIG, neocloud, FinOps-dashboard, Kueue-preemption-only (fix 4) |
| 11 | Go / no-go | §8 | fix "shrink = clean win" → irreversibility; add batch-inf utilization as go-signal |
| — | Platform-gap note | NEW | flag: value case now depends on a **3rd priority class** the platform doesn't model yet — prerequisite/roadmap |

### Accuracy guards while writing
- **MIG characterized precisely** — hardware-isolated but *static* partition, fragments the GPU, can't serve large-NVLink training; a real competitor for small co-located inference+experiments, not for the whole-fleet training case. Not a strawman, not overblown. (I'll verify current MIG state if any claim feels shaky.)
- **All $ figures stay explicitly illustrative** — keep the existing disclaimer; add batch-inf revenue as a *lever*, not a fabricated number.
- **No slop** — ai-writing-tropes rules apply.

### Deliverable
Rewritten `docs/value-case.md`, plus a one-line commit-ready summary. No commit/push unless you ask.

**Two open flags before I write:**
1. **Numbers** — I have no batch-inference volume or revenue for the internal BU. I'll keep everything as *illustrative levers* (the doc already does this). OK, or do you have real figures to seed?
2. **Platform-gap note** — include the "needs a 3rd priority class" prerequisite in the value case, or keep the doc pure-business and log the platform gap separately?

Approve as-is, or adjust — then I write it.

input.content:
# Value case — why run the pilot

For the Head of Platform deciding whether synorg earns a real pilot. Its deeper
job is to arm the decision that comes *after* the pilot — the production
keep-or-shrink call on a large standing cost, which Finance owns. So the framing
here is deliberately finance-legible, even though the pilot itself is a
platform-team call.

The honest version up front: lending is not a payback machine, and pretending
otherwise fails the first finance review. The value is measured de-risking and
governance of a large standing cost you are already paying, plus — for a customer
serving external businesses — a provable data-isolation control.

All dollar figures below are **illustrative**, computed from the pilot held book
in ADR 0005. Swap your own fleet, window, and prices; the arithmetic and its
levers are what matter, not these specific numbers.

## 1. The workload reality — three classes, not two

synorg serves an internal customer: a business unit running B2B services. That
customer puts three workload classes on the held fleet, and they differ on two
axes that do **not** move together — SLA hardness and trust domain:

| Workload | SLA | Trust domain |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Realtime inference** (interactive B2B API) | hard (ms), spiky, diurnal | customer data |
| **Batch inference** (async generation, e.g. text-to-video) | soft (deadline, mins–hrs), revenue | customer data — same as realtime |
| **R&D training** (model development) | softest (best-effort, internal) | different — must be scrubbed |

The split is the whole game. Batch inference is soft-SLA but shares realtime's
trust domain, so a node moving between them needs no scrub. R&D training is also
soft-SLA but crosses the trust boundary: it must never retain a byte of
customer-inference GPU memory, so a node handed from inference to training and
back has to be scrubbed. Everything below follows from those two facts.

## 2. The contradiction, and how synorg resolves it

The held fleet embodies a physical contradiction. The same scarce GPU capacity
must be **dedicated to inference** — to hold a latency SLA you cannot buy on
demand — and **used by other work**, or you are paying to hold it idle. There are
only a handful of ways to resolve that:

- **Separate capacity (space).** Buy distinct fleets for each. This is the status
  quo, and the double-pay.
- **Time.** Same GPU, inference when it needs it, other work when it doesn't.
  synorg's core move.
- **Condition (priority).** One pool, inference preempts everything on demand.
  Plain Kueue / kube-scheduler priority.
- **Silicon (MIG).** Partition one GPU into hardware-isolated slices, run both at
  once.
- **Market.** Rent pooled capacity from a neocloud and let someone else hold the
  idle.

synorg uses **time-separation for the cross-domain (R&D) work, plus a warm floor
it never lends** for the unpredictable inference spikes. Why not the simpler
condition-preemption? Because eviction across the trust boundary is not instant.
R&D training must checkpoint, drain, and the GPU must be scrubbed before inference
can safely reuse it — none of which fits inside the milliseconds a latency SLA
allows. So synorg reclaims *ahead* of predicted demand (before the morning ramp)
rather than reacting to it, and the warm floor absorbs whatever the prediction
misses.

That resolution only earns its complexity where three preconditions hold:

1. **GPU is scarce** — you cannot buy inference capacity on demand, so releasing
   the fleet is not reversible. In 2026 this is market reality: p5/H100 on-demand
   is effectively sold out, and capacity reservations bought in advance are the
   only guaranteed path.
2. **The trust boundary is real** — naive pooling of R&D training with
   customer-data inference violates isolation the customer is contractually on the
   hook for.
3. **Scrub and checkpoint latency are nonzero** — you cannot preempt reactively
   fast enough to protect a hard SLA.

Remove any one and a simpler resolution wins (abundant capacity → buy per use;
soft boundary → one Kueue pool; instant isolation → MIG in silicon). Those three
preconditions are the honest scope of when synorg is the right tool.

## 3. The cost being attacked

Scarce GPU is rented and deliberately never descaled, because released capacity
may not return. Two bills land every month, both inside one internal customer's
budget:

- **Held-fleet burn** — the reserved book bills at full on-demand whether used or
  not. The pilot book (4× p5.48xlarge, 8× g6e.12xlarge, 3× g7e.2xlarge) is on the
  order of **$350k/month** of standing burn (ADR 0005).
- **Separately-bought training** — R&D trains on its own GPUs while the inference
  fleet sits idle overnight. You pay the availability premium on the held fleet
  *and* full price for training capacity. The double-pay, and here it is entirely
  internal — one customer paying both bills.

## 4. What synorg recovers — the priority stack

At any moment the held fleet's capacity is one of three things:

1. **Serving inference.** During the day, most of it — the fleet is sized for the
   daytime peak, which is its job.
2. **Surge headroom held on purpose.** The warm floor, insurance against a spike
   you cannot buy your way out of. This idle is not recoverable; it is the product.
3. **Idle long enough to fill safely.** Capacity that will stay free long enough
   to reclaim ahead of demand.

Only bucket 3 is recoverable, and it fills as a **priority stack ordered by
value**:

- **Realtime inference** always wins.
- **Batch inference** fills next. Same trust domain as realtime, so handing a node
  back needs no scrub — just checkpoint and requeue. That makes it safe to soak
  *daytime* troughs, not only overnight, with plain priority scheduling and no
  lending machinery at all. Its value per GPU-hour is customer revenue (or avoided
  batch capacity), not a training substitute. And if the customer runs a batch B2B
  product, this demand is endogenous and near-certain — not the speculative
  backlog the recovery math usually worries about.
- **R&D training** fills the residual overnight idle. This is the cross-domain
  slice that actually needs synorg's lending controller, scrub, and
  reclaim-before-ramp, priced at the training-substitute rate.

The illustrative table below is that **R&D-residual slice alone** — the smallest,
last filler on the stack, from ~35 lendable GPUs across an 8.5-hour window:

| Utilization of the lendable window | Training GPU-hours captured /mo | Value recovered /mo | Share of the $350k burn |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 50% | ~4,500 | ~$7–11k | ~2–3% |
| 70% (the ADR 0005 floor) | ~6,250 | ~$9–16k | ~3–5% |
| 90% | ~8,000 | ~$12–20k | ~3–6% |

(Training price band $1.5–2.5/GPU-hr, L40S-class.) Read correctly: the
single-digit-% figure is the *training residual*, not the recovery. Priced across
the whole stack — batch inference at revenue on top of R&D at substitution — the
recoverable value is materially larger and far more certain. **Lending does not
pay for the fleet.** The priority stack, led by the customer's own batch inference,
is where the real recovery is; R&D lending is the last slice, not the headline.

## 5. What synorg does *not* do

The boundary matters as much as the claim:

- **Daytime simultaneous R&D demand is out of scope.** synorg time-shifts R&D
  training into inference's idle hours. It does not hand R&D guaranteed daytime
  capacity. A researcher who needs a GPU at 2 p.m. runs on R&D's own capacity or
  queues for the window.
- **Daytime idle is not a lending target.** When inference is off-peak mid-day,
  that spare capacity is either surge headroom you must keep or evidence the fleet
  is oversized. Neither is something to lend; the second is a signal to *shrink*.
- **Recoverable value equals the overlap** between inference's idle hours and
  time-flexible demand (batch inference plus R&D backlog). No overlap, no recovery.
  That overlap is a property of the customer's workload rhythm — which the pilot
  measures rather than assumes.

## 6. What actually justifies the held book

The fleet is affordable for reasons lending only supplements:

- **SLO insurance.** p5/H100-class capacity cannot be provisioned on demand; the
  warm floor is bought so customer inference has guaranteed render-start latency.
  The primary justification, and it exists with or without lending.
- **Commitment discounts.** Savings Plans and RIs stack on top of capacity
  reservations (ADR 0005). This is where most of the affordability comes from; the
  ledger review verifies coverage.
- **Compliance isolation.** The scrub-on-return and the zero-net-release ledger
  are not only reliability mechanisms. For a customer serving external businesses
  under a data-protection contract, they are an auditable control — per-cycle
  evidence that training never retained a byte of customer-inference memory,
  something the customer can show its own auditors and clients.
- **Lending margin.** The residual recovery above — real, measured, marginal
  against the burn.

## 7. The pilot's real value: de-risk and govern

Today the $350k/month is spent **blind** — nothing measures whether it earns its
keep. synorg makes lending *safe* (the walking skeleton proved lend → reclaim →
scrub → rejoin with zero-net-release and inference latency held), which turns a
standing cost into a governed one via two numbers:

- **`utilization-of-held`** — GPU-hours allocated ÷ held, per pool. Below the 70%
  floor for a rolling month → shrink the reservation, widen the window, or
  re-justify the sizing in writing. One caveat: shrinking a held GPU book is a
  **one-way door** — in a shortage the released capacity may not return at any
  price — so a shrink is a deliberate bet the measurement must inform, not a
  reflex.
- **`idle-burn`** — $/day of held-but-unallocated capacity at on-demand rates. The
  premium the lending machine offsets, tracked instead of assumed.

Because the customer is internal, these two metrics are also a **chargeback
lever**: the platform can allocate the held cost to the BU by measured use and
hold it accountable for its own sizing. De-risking becomes FinOps governance.

The payoff is not the ~$12k/month of lending revenue. It is that **you stop paying
$350k/month on faith and start paying it on evidence** — with a safe lever
(lending) and, for a B2B customer, a compliance proof (the ledger) on top.

Gate the whole case on one premise: **you cannot shrink the insurance.** If you
could, you would measure and shrink and skip the platform — a FinOps dashboard
measures `utilization-of-held` for a fraction of the cost. But a dashboard can
only tell you the fleet is idle; it cannot act. The only safe action on idle you
must keep is to lend it. Because the warm floor is surge insurance you are keeping
regardless, lending is the sole way to recover the idle without giving up the
guarantee.

## 8. Break-even, stated plainly

The economic claim (ADR 0005) is:

```
training GPU-hours captured + surge readiness  ≥  held cost − commitment discounts
```

Solved honestly: **commitment discounts and SLO-insurance value carry the
inequality; the priority stack (batch inference first, then R&D lending) and surge
readiness close the gap and, more importantly, make the left side observable and
adjustable.** With batch inference on the stack, the left side is both larger and
more certain than a training-only reading suggests. Lending is the mechanism that
makes the left side measurable, not the mechanism that makes it large.

## 9. Levers (what the pilot lets you tune)

- **Lend-window width** — longer window, more capture, but less warm-floor slack
  before the morning ramp.
- **Lendable fraction** — how much of the held book above the warm floor is
  offered; trades surge readiness for capture.
- **Batch-vs-training mix** — how much idle goes to revenue-priced batch inference
  versus training-substitute R&D. The highest-value knob on the stack.
- **Tier boundaries** — where the realtime / batch / R&D priorities sit, and which
  classes share a trust domain (scrub or no scrub).
- **Utilization floor** — the 70% target that triggers shrink / widen / re-justify.
- **Commitment coverage** — the largest affordability lever, verified each ledger
  review.

## 10. Why not just…

The real alternatives a technical and financial room raises, answered:

- **…buy a FinOps dashboard?** It measures `utilization-of-held` and `idle-burn`
  for a fraction of the cost — but it can only report that the fleet is idle, not
  act on it. The only safe action on idle you must keep is to lend it; a dashboard
  leaves the idle burning.
- **…use MIG to partition each GPU in silicon?** MIG gives hardware-isolated
  slices on one physical GPU simultaneously, which is genuinely useful for
  co-locating small inference with small experiments and sidesteps scrub latency.
  But MIG partitions are static and fragment the GPU — each slice is a fraction, so
  a large training run that wants a whole 8×H100 node with NVLink cannot use it,
  and repartitioning still drains the GPU. MIG fits the small co-location case; it
  does not serve the whole-fleet overnight-training case. Where it fits, use it —
  it is complementary, not a replacement.
- **…rent from a neocloud?** Let someone else hold the idle and rent
  dedicated-feeling capacity. A real option where available and compliant, but it
  moves the customer's data to a third party (a contractual problem for a B2B
  service under a DPA), and in a shortage the on-demand capacity you are counting
  on is exactly what is sold out.
- **…run one pool with Kueue priority preemption, no lending layer?** That works
  *within* a trust domain — it is precisely how batch inference should soak idle.
  It does not work *across* the R&D boundary: preemption cannot checkpoint, drain,
  and scrub inside a latency SLA's budget, so training on the shared pool either
  risks the SLO or never yields safely. The lending layer exists for exactly the
  case priority-preemption cannot cover.
- **…do nothing?** The status quo the pilot exists to end: $350k/month spent on
  faith, no `utilization-of-held`, no lever, no compliance proof. "Do nothing" is
  not free — it is the blind cost, indefinitely.

## 11. Go / no-go for the pilot

A right-sized pilot (one region, the cheap profile the e2e harness already runs —
on the order of days and low-hundreds of dollars in cloud, plus the platform
team's evaluation time) decides:

- **Go to production if:** lending runs safely at target scale (reclaim beats the
  ramp deadline, inference p95 holds, ledger zero-net-release — all demonstrated in
  the walking skeleton), **and** `utilization-of-held` trends toward the 70% floor
  once real batch-inference and training demand is pointed at it.
- **Shrink the book instead if:** utilization stays well below floor — the pilot
  has then earned its keep by telling you the hold is oversized. Note the shrink is
  itself irreversible in a shortage, so treat it as a deliberate, measured bet, not
  a costless win.
- **Kill if:** lending can't hold the inference SLO under real load (it did in
  rehearsal; the pilot tests it under your traffic).

Either way the pilot converts a blind standing cost into a measured, governed, and
— for a B2B customer — provably-isolated one. That is the value; the lending
revenue is a bonus on top.

---

## Prerequisite — the platform must model three tiers

This case assumes the fleet can express three workload classes. The platform today
models **two** priority tiers (`inference-critical`, `training-preemptible`). The
batch-inference tier — preemptible but above training, and same trust domain so it
needs no scrub — is a required increment before the batch-inference recovery in §4
is real. It is flagged here because the value case depends on it, and it is a small
platform change (a third `PriorityClass` and a Kueue tier), not a redesign.

toolUseResult.content:
# Value case — why run the pilot

For the Head of Platform deciding whether synorg earns a real pilot. Its deeper
job is to arm the decision that comes *after* the pilot — the production
keep-or-shrink call on a large standing cost, which Finance owns. So the framing
here is deliberately finance-legible, even though the pilot itself is a
platform-team call.

The honest version up front: lending is not a payback machine, and pretending
otherwise fails the first finance review. The value is measured de-risking and
governance of a large standing cost you are already paying, plus — for a customer
serving external businesses — a provable data-isolation control.

All dollar figures below are **illustrative**, computed from the pilot held book
in ADR 0005. Swap your own fleet, window, and prices; the arithmetic and its
levers are what matter, not these specific numbers.

## 1. The workload reality — three classes, not two

synorg serves an internal customer: a business unit running B2B services. That
customer puts three workload classes on the held fleet, and they differ on two
axes that do **not** move together — SLA hardness and trust domain:

| Workload | SLA | Trust domain |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Realtime inference** (interactive B2B API) | hard (ms), spiky, diurnal | customer data |
| **Batch inference** (async generation, e.g. text-to-video) | soft (deadline, mins–hrs), revenue | customer data — same as realtime |
| **R&D training** (model development) | softest (best-effort, internal) | different — must be scrubbed |

The split is the whole game. Batch inference is soft-SLA but shares realtime's
trust domain, so a node moving between them needs no scrub. R&D training is also
soft-SLA but crosses the trust boundary: it must never retain a byte of
customer-inference GPU memory, so a node handed from inference to training and
back has to be scrubbed. Everything below follows from those two facts.

## 2. The contradiction, and how synorg resolves it

The held fleet embodies a physical contradiction. The same scarce GPU capacity
must be **dedicated to inference** — to hold a latency SLA you cannot buy on
demand — and **used by other work**, or you are paying to hold it idle. There are
only a handful of ways to resolve that:

- **Separate capacity (space).** Buy distinct fleets for each. This is the status
  quo, and the double-pay.
- **Time.** Same GPU, inference when it needs it, other work when it doesn't.
  synorg's core move.
- **Condition (priority).** One pool, inference preempts everything on demand.
  Plain Kueue / kube-scheduler priority.
- **Silicon (MIG).** Partition one GPU into hardware-isolated slices, run both at
  once.
- **Market.** Rent pooled capacity from a neocloud and let someone else hold the
  idle.

synorg uses **time-separation for the cross-domain (R&D) work, plus a warm floor
it never lends** for the unpredictable inference spikes. Why not the simpler
condition-preemption? Because eviction across the trust boundary is not instant.
R&D training must checkpoint, drain, and the GPU must be scrubbed before inference
can safely reuse it — none of which fits inside the milliseconds a latency SLA
allows. So synorg reclaims *ahead* of predicted demand (before the morning ramp)
rather than reacting to it, and the warm floor absorbs whatever the prediction
misses.

That resolution only earns its complexity where three preconditions hold:

1. **GPU is scarce** — you cannot buy inference capacity on demand, so releasing
   the fleet is not reversible. In 2026 this is market reality: p5/H100 on-demand
   is effectively sold out, and capacity reservations bought in advance are the
   only guaranteed path.
2. **The trust boundary is real** — naive pooling of R&D training with
   customer-data inference violates isolation the customer is contractually on the
   hook for.
3. **Scrub and checkpoint latency are nonzero** — you cannot preempt reactively
   fast enough to protect a hard SLA.

Remove any one and a simpler resolution wins (abundant capacity → buy per use;
soft boundary → one Kueue pool; instant isolation → MIG in silicon). Those three
preconditions are the honest scope of when synorg is the right tool.

## 3. The cost being attacked

Scarce GPU is rented and deliberately never descaled, because released capacity
may not return. Two bills land every month, both inside one internal customer's
budget:

- **Held-fleet burn** — the reserved book bills at full on-demand whether used or
  not. The pilot book (4× p5.48xlarge, 8× g6e.12xlarge, 3× g7e.2xlarge) is on the
  order of **$350k/month** of standing burn (ADR 0005).

  fleet sits idle overnight. You pay the availability premium on the held fleet

  internal — one customer paying both bills.

+must be **dedicated to inference** — to hold a latency SLA you cannot buy on
+demand — and **used by other work**, or you are paying to hold it idle. There are
+only a handful of ways to resolve that:
+- **Separate capacity (space).** Buy distinct fleets for each. This is the status
+  quo, and the double-pay.
+- **Time.** Same GPU, inference when it needs it, other work when it doesn't.
+  synorg's core move.
+- **Condition (priority).** One pool, inference preempts everything on demand.
+  Plain Kueue / kube-scheduler priority.
+- **Silicon (MIG).** Partition one GPU into hardware-isolated slices, run both at
+  once.
+- **Market.** Rent pooled capacity from a neocloud and let someone else hold the
+  idle.
+synorg uses **time-separation for the cross-domain (R&D) work, plus a warm floor
+it never lends** for the unpredictable inference spikes. Why not the simpler
+condition-preemption? Because eviction across the trust boundary is not instant.
+R&D training must checkpoint, drain, and the GPU must be scrubbed before inference
+can safely reuse it — none of which fits inside the milliseconds a latency SLA
+allows. So synorg reclaims *ahead* of predicted demand (before the morning ramp)
+rather than reacting to it, and the warm floor absorbs whatever the prediction
+misses.
+That resolution only earns its complexity where three preconditions hold:
+1. **GPU is scarce** — you cannot buy inference capacity on demand, so releasing
+   the fleet is not reversible. In 2026 this is market reality: p5/H100 on-demand
+   is effectively sold out, and capacity reservations bought in advance are the
+   only guaranteed path.
+2. **The trust boundary is real** — naive pooling of R&D training with
+   customer-data inference violates isolation the customer is contractually on the
+   hook for.
+3. **Scrub and checkpoint latency are nonzero** — you cannot preempt reactively
+   fast enough to protect a hard SLA.
+Remove any one and a simpler resolution wins (abundant capacity → buy per use;
+soft boundary → one Kueue pool; instant isolation → MIG in silicon). Those three
+preconditions are the honest scope of when synorg is the right tool.

+may not return. Two bills land every month, both inside one internal customer's
+budget:
+- **Held-fleet burn** — the reserved book bills at full on-demand whether used or
not. The pilot book (4× p5.48xlarge, 8× g6e.12xlarge, 3× g7e.2xlarge) is on the
order of **$350k/month** of standing burn (ADR 0005).

-  the held fleet *and* full price for training capacity — the double‑pay.

+  internal — one customer paying both bills.

# Value case — why run the pilot

For the Head of Platform deciding whether synorg earns a real pilot. The honest
version: lending is not a payback machine, and pretending otherwise fails the
first finance review. The value is measured de‑risking of a large standing cost
you are already paying.

All dollar figures below are **illustrative**, computed from the pilot held book
in ADR 0005 at eu‑west‑1 on‑demand rates. Swap your own fleet, window, and
training price — the arithmetic and its levers are what matter, not these
specific numbers.

## 1. The cost being attacked

Scarce GPU capacity is rented and deliberately never descaled (released capacity
may not return). Two bills land every month:

- **Held‑fleet burn** — the reserved book bills at full on‑demand whether used or
  not. The pilot book (4× p5.48xlarge, 8× g6e.12xlarge, 3× g7e.2xlarge) is on the
  order of **$350k/month** of standing burn (ADR 0005).
- **Separately‑bought training** — R&D trains at 100% on its own GPUs while the
  inference fleet sits ~90% idle overnight. You pay the availability premium on
  the held fleet *and* full price for training capacity — the double‑pay.

## 2. What lending directly recovers — be honest, it's a slice

Lending captures overnight‑idle **lendable** GPUs (the g6e/g7e portion — the p5
warm floor is inference insurance and is never lent) for training that would
otherwise be bought separately. From the pilot book, ~35 lendable GPUs × the
8.5‑hour lend window:

| Utilization of the lendable window | Training GPU‑hours captured /mo | Value recovered /mo | Share of the $350k burn |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 50% | ~4,500 | ~$7–11k | ~2–3% |
| 70% (the ADR 0005 floor) | ~6,250 | ~$9–16k | ~3–5% |
| 90% | ~8,000 | ~$12–20k | ~3–6% |

(Training price band $1.5–2.5/GPU‑hr, L40S‑class.) **Lending does not pay for the
fleet.** Anyone who claims it does hasn't done this arithmetic.

### What this model does not capture — and why the pilot exists

The table above is a **single‑region, uniform‑workload floor of understanding**,
not a forecast. Three drivers dominate the real answer, and none are modeled here
because none are knowable without running it:

- **Inference demand shape.** "~90% idle overnight" is an average, not a
  guarantee. Real inference load is lumpy — spikes, timezone spread, launch
  events. Every hour inference actually needs the lendable pool is an hour it
  can't be lent. The clean 22:00–06:30 window is an upper bound on availability.
- **R&D backlog alignment.** The 70% utilization number *assumes R&D always has
  overnight work to fill 35 lent GPUs.* If the training backlog is thin, bursty,
  or deadline‑misaligned with the window, `utilization‑of‑held` craters and the
  recovery with it. This is the single largest swing factor, and it is a property
  of *your* R&D org, not the platform.
- **Regional arbitrage.** The core rationale (ADR 0001) is that GPU availability,
  instance families, and spot/reservation dynamics differ *between regions* — so
  work is placed where availability is. This model is single‑region and captures
  none of it: follow‑the‑sun inference peaks, R&D data gravity that may not
  co‑locate with idle capacity, MultiKueue cross‑region dispatch. The multi‑region
  value could be materially higher or lower; the arithmetic here does not reach it.

This is precisely why the point estimate is not the pitch. **The pilot's job is to
measure the joint distribution of (inference demand × R&D backlog × regional
availability) that this model assumes away** — turning a floor‑of‑understanding
into a real one. A value case that claimed to already know these numbers would be
the thing to distrust.

## 3. What actually justifies the held book

The fleet is affordable for reasons lending only *supplements*:

- **SLO insurance.** p5/H100‑class capacity cannot be provisioned on demand; the
  warm floor is bought so customer inference has guaranteed render‑start latency.
  That's the primary justification, and it exists with or without lending.
- **Commitment discounts.** Savings Plans / RIs stack on top of capacity
  reservations (ADR 0005). Holding without them pays for insurance twice; the
  ledger review verifies coverage. This is where most of the "affordability"
  comes from.
- **Lending margin.** The single‑digit‑% recovery above — real, measured, but
  marginal against the burn.

Read together: the hold is insurance you're paying anyway, made cheaper at the
margin by lending, and — critically — **now measured**.

## 4. The pilot's real value: de‑risking a blind $350k/month decision

This is the line that greenlights the pilot. Today the $350k/month held cost is
spent **blind** — nothing measures whether it earns its keep. synorg makes lending
*safe* (the walking skeleton proved lend → reclaim → scrub → rejoin with
zero‑net‑release and inference latency held), which turns two numbers into
actionable levers:

- **`utilization‑of‑held`** — GPU‑hours allocated ÷ held, per pool. Below the 70%
  floor for a rolling month → the standing options are **shrink the reservation,
  widen the lending window, or re‑justify the sizing in writing.**
- **`idle‑burn`** — $/day of held‑but‑unallocated capacity at on‑demand rates.
  The premium the lending machine is offsetting, tracked instead of assumed.

**The pilot's payoff is not the ~$12k/month of lending revenue. It is that you
stop paying $350k/month on faith and start paying it on evidence** — with a lever
(safe lending) to act on the evidence. De‑risking a decision that size is worth
far more than the marginal recovery.

## 5. Break‑even, stated plainly

The economic claim (ADR 0005) is:

```
training GPU‑hours captured + surge readiness  ≥  held cost − commitment discounts
```

Solved honestly: **commitment discounts + SLO‑insurance value carry the
inequality; lending margin + surge readiness close the remaining gap and, more
importantly, provide the measurement that keeps the whole book honest over time.**
Lending is the mechanism that makes the left side *observable and adjustable*, not
the mechanism that makes it large.

## 6. Levers (what the pilot lets you tune)

- **Lend‑window width** — longer window, more capture, but less warm-floor slack
  before the morning ramp.
- **Lendable fraction** — how much of the held book above the warm floor is
  offered; trades surge readiness for capture.
- **Utilization floor** — the 70% target that triggers shrink/widen/re‑justify.
- **Commitment coverage** — the largest affordability lever, verified each ledger
  review.

## 7. Why not just…

The first questions in the room, answered:

- **…train on pure spot?** Spot is cheap but it's *someone else's* preemption
  clock — AWS reclaims on its schedule, not before your morning ramp, so you
  can't guarantee inference headroom when you need it. And it does nothing about
  the held inference fleet, which still sits idle overnight. You'd have cheap
  training and an unmeasured $350k burn side by side. (ADR 0005 rejects
  hold‑warm‑floor + pure‑spot for exactly this — it guts surge readiness.)
- **…buy more dedicated GPUs for training?** That widens the double‑pay into a
  triple‑pay: held inference + existing training + more training, with the idle
  burn untouched. It scales cost linearly and never asks whether the capacity you
  already hold is earning its keep.
- **…use Karpenter + Kueue without the lending layer?** They provision and admit,
  but nothing makes lend → reclaim *safe against the inference SLO* — draining
  before the ramp, scrubbing to a genuinely new instance, proving zero‑net
  capacity release. That orchestration, and the measurement it enables, is the
  product; the schedulers are ingredients.
- **…do nothing?** That's the status quo the pilot exists to end: $350k/month
  spent on faith, no `utilization‑of‑held`, no lever. "Do nothing" is not free —
  it's the blind cost, indefinitely.

## 8. Go / no‑go for the pilot

A right‑sized pilot (one region, the cheap profile the e2e harness already runs —
on the order of days and low‑hundreds of dollars in cloud, plus the platform
team's evaluation time) decides:

- **Go to production if:** lending runs safely at target scale (reclaim beats the
  ramp deadline, inference p95 holds, ledger zero‑net‑release — all demonstrated
  in the walking skeleton), **and** `utilization‑of‑held` trends toward the 70%
  floor once real training backlog is pointed at it.
- **Shrink the book instead if:** utilization stays well below floor — the pilot
  has then earned its keep by telling you the hold is oversized, which is a
  win, not a failure.
- **Kill if:** lending can't hold the inference SLO under real load (it did in
  rehearsal; the pilot tests it under your traffic).

Either way the pilot converts a blind standing cost into a measured one. That is
the value — the lending revenue is a bonus on top.
